paragraphs.body: Nothing seems to be more frustrating in life than when little things just seem to go wrong. Your text messages aren’t sending, your TV show is buffering, or your delivery order was missing your side of ranch. Anytime these things happened to me I got annoyed and wondered to myself why something so simple couldn’t be as easy as it sounds. All of these little things would add up and I would feel like the world was plotting against me. I would think to myself, “Why can’t life just be simple and everything work out?” It wouldn’t be until I experienced true hardship that those little things seemed to truly be little and insignificant. This experience endowed upon me the simplicity of our lives. I guess it just took me taking on a difficult challenge to realize just how easy the rest of my life was.

annotations.text: Your text messages aren’t sending, your TV show is buffering, or your delivery order was missing your side of ranch.
annotations.comment: nice voice and tone. :) I wonder, though, if you should start your essay with your narrative? What if these details came later?

paragraphs.body: As I walk through my parent’s backyard, I can never help but notice the changing of the seasons. The trees are spackled with an array of colors as fall approaches. Leaves tumbling down like feathers thrown from a torn pillow. When these leaves are piled high, they become a cushion for kids to protect them from the hard ground as they plummet into them. When I walk along the tree line, the leaves beneath my feet crunch like a bite into a freshly picked apple. The chill in the air as fall approaches gives me the feeling of a chapter coming to an end. As I look out into the sea of trees beyond my parent’s house, I witness the leaves all seemingly in different stages of their lives. Some are even still greener than the grass and thriving. They are strongly connected to their home. Other leaves are red and orange and are still hanging on, but coming closer to the ground beneath them. The most unfortunate of them all, the brown leaves withering away as nature had intended. They lay in a sea of others and wait to be blown far away from the only place they’ve ever known. These leaves had withstood the wind gusts all year, yet it just takes one singular whisp of air at just the right time to separate them from the home they’ve grown so close to. I suppose it was my turn to be blown away from my home. I had enlisted in the military and my ship date was today.

annotations.text: suppose it was my turn to be blown away from my home. I had enlisted in the military and my ship date was today.

annotations.comment: Should this paragraph be in past tense? And could this whole experience be in a narrative about the last walk you took in your neighborhood/yard before getting in the car for boot camp.
annotations.context_left: seasons. The trees are spackled with an array of colors as fall approaches. Leaves tumbling down

paragraphs.body: As I was leaving for boot camp, I walked to my car parked at my parent’s house and couldn’t help but to take in the fall scenery around their house. The sun is beginning to set behind the painting of trees in the distance. The trees swaying in the wind that is beginning to pick up. I can feel the chill in the air on my exposed skin as I place my hands into the warmth of my pockets. As I breathe in the fresh and chilly air, I get a feeling of readiness. A readiness to take on an impending challenge that will soon dawn upon me. I will have to drift away from this comforting scene just as the leaves must tumble away from their birthplace. Instead of leaving and knowing I will be back by the end of the night, I would be driving away and having to look at the changing trees and the simple house all alone on the hillside one last time for the foreseeable future. I was leaving for South Carolina and knew there was a chance that I may never see this beautiful and comforting scene again. Because of this memory, I soak in every chance I get since that moment to take in the familiar sight and never forget how grateful I am to be able to know that I will be able to see it next time.

annotations.context_right: couldn’t help but to take in the fall scenery around their house. The sun is beginning to set

annotations.comment: I wonder if this paragraph is necessary for your essay? What if, instead of this explanation, you took your reader (metaphorically) to boot camp with you and showed them the challenge of that experience with a descriptive paragraph?
annotations.context_right: my pockets. As I breathe in the fresh and chilly air, I get a feeling of readiness. A readiness to

paragraphs.body: People are faced with challenges in an unequal amount throughout our lives. A challenge is not when your favorite coffee shop runs out of the creamer you like or when you can’t decide which restaurant to order from for dinner. A challenge is when a life altering decision or task is in your path and you must decide how to navigate it in order to have the outcome you desire. A challenge is when you are in a firefight and must choose whether to order your men to continue advancing or freeze. It is when you and your family are starving and you must find food by any means necessary in order for your family to survive. The term commonly thrown around is a first world problem. This idea plagues everyone involved with thoughts of what they think a real challenge is. Instead of having a feeling of gratefulness to be unbothered by the world’s deepest issues on a daily basis, we create our own problems and perceived hardships. Having to wait 20 minutes longer than expected for your prepared pizza made exactly how you like it and it being hand delivered to you happens to be some people’s hardest part of their day. A sense of ungratefulness for what we have is the bane of our country’s issues. Nobody ever seems to stop and think just how great we have it.

annotations.text: sense of ungratefulness for what we have is the bane of our country’s issues. Nobody ever seems to stop and think just how great we have
annotations.comment: Who is "nobody" here? Who and what, specifically, are you noticing?

I'm interested in this definition paragraph. Great details. I wonder, though, if you can leverage it to speak more directly to your essay's central focusing idea? Let's talk about who your essay's audience and purpose are.
annotations.context_left: challenge is when you are in a firefight and must choose whether to order your men to continue

paragraphs.body: This realization dawned on me when I left for the military and took on the hardest four years I have ever experienced. I have experienced sleeping outside in negative temperatures for months on end, hiking with over 100 pounds on my back traversing mountains covered in six feet of snow, going without sleep for multiple days and hallucinating as I sit on watch, and numerous other hardships. This time truly put challenges and life into perspective for me. We are all so blissfully unaware of how difficult life truly can be. I have only had a taste of genuine hard living and would not wish that lifestyle on my worst enemy. Today, you could never leave your home all of your life and pass on from simply just old age. All of these things are fantastic and show the improvements to life standards we have made; however, there should not be a day that goes by where you are not grateful for the comfortable and safe life you can live. Being thankful that you do not have to hunt for survival, be concerned whether you will freeze to death at night, or be killed in your sleep by an enemy. There is always someone who has it worse than you. This idea is hard to conceptualize in your mind, but it is a simple fact of life. I would hear this saying all the time when I complained about something that did not really matter. I didn’t particularly care that others had it harder than me because I didn’t know them, but I was obliviously ignorant to the real struggles of life since I had never experienced anything close to it. Now, this old saying rings true for me and it should ring true for everyone. So, take a step back, take in the changing of the leaves and the absolute blissful harmony of the trees swaying in the wind, and realize that life isn’t so bad.

annotations.text: This realization dawned on me when I left for the military and took on the hardest four years I have ever experienced. I have experienced sleeping outside in negative temperatures for months on end, hiking with over 100 pounds on my back traversing mountains covered in six feet of snow, going without sleep for multiple days and hallucinating as I sit on watch, and numerous other hardships.
annotations.comment: These details (which are great), for example, could go earlier in the essay (see my notes about boot camp and challenge).

Being thankful that you do not have to hunt for survival, be concerned whether you will freeze to death at night, or be killed in your sleep by an enemy.
